famous_works.notes: A Sunday afternoon at the outdoor dance hall in Montmartre. The dappled Impressionist light applied to a scene of bourgeois leisure — one of the movement's defining images. Musée d'Orsay.

famous_works.title: Luncheon of the Boating Party
famous_works.notes: Renoir's friends lunching on a terrace above the Seine at Chatou. The most elaborate of his outdoor figure compositions — Impressionist technique at its most socially immersive.

famous_works.title: The Large Bathers
famous_works.notes: A monumental outdoor nude composition in a more classical style than the Impressionist period — Renoir's attempt to reconcile plein-air observation with the grand tradition.

lesser_known_works.title: The Swing
lesser_known_works.notes: A small painting made in his garden in Montmartre — the same location as Le Moulin. The dappled light effects are even more immediate here than in the larger canvas.

lesser_known_works.title: Young Women at the Piano
lesser_known_works.notes: Commissioned by the French state for the Luxembourg Museum. The domestic interior at its most comfortable — Renoir's bourgeois optimism at its least problematically cheerful.

lesser_known_works.title: The Theatre Box
lesser_known_works.notes: A woman in a theatre box, the male companion a dark background figure. The woman as spectacle within the spectacle of the theatre — the looking and being-looked-at doubled.

top_lot.title: Vache dans un verger
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

artists.bio: Born in Philadelphia, Whitney studied at the Kansas City Art Institute and the Yale School of Art before moving to New York and then Rome, where he has spent significant periods of his career. He developed his distinctive grid-based abstract painting in Rome in the 1970s: a format of stacked horizontal bands, each containing vertical colour blocks, which he has explored with increasing depth since the 1990s. The grid is fixed but the colour relationships within it are endlessly varied — warm against cool, saturated against muted, advancing against receding. He cites Paul Klee, Miles Davis, and jazz improvisation as structural influences. His market was modest until the late 2010s when a broader revaluation of mid-career Black American abstractionists created rapid appreciation. His work is now in the collections of MoMA and the Whitney.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Venice)
famous_works.notes: A large vertical grid of warm and cool colour blocks — Whitney's mature language at its most confident. Made following his Venice Biennale presentation.

famous_works.title: Blue Nile
famous_works.notes: Named for the Ethiopian song. The blues of the title — music, geography, colour — converge in a canvas of extraordinary chromatic complexity within the constrained grid format.

famous_works.title: Dance the Orange
famous_works.notes: Named for the Rilke poem. The formal vocabulary of the grid freed into a looser organisation — the grid as improvisation rather than constraint.

lesser_known_works.title: Jazz
lesser_known_works.notes: An early mature work that establishes the stacked grid format. The jazz reference — improvisation within a fixed structure — is the method before it is the title.

lesser_known_works.title: Slow Boat to China
lesser_known_works.notes: A mid-career work in deep blues and greens. The long horizontal format that Whitney uses to extend the rhythm of the colour bands.

lesser_known_works.title: American Painting
lesser_known_works.notes: A canvas in which red, white, and blue — the obvious national palette — are distributed across the grid without irony. The American tradition absorbed and answered rather than quoted.

artists.bio: Born in Brooklyn, Longo studied at the Nova Scotia College of Art and Design and at the State University of New York at Buffalo, where he co-founded Hallwalls (1974) with Cindy Sherman and Charles Clough. His Men in the Cities drawings (1976–83) — monumental charcoal figures in business suits, frozen in convulsive contortions as if struck by an invisible force — became the defining images of 1980s urban alienation. The figures were projected from photographs of friends and traced, then drawn with obsessive hyperrealist precision. His more recent large-scale charcoal works — atomic bomb detonations, ocean waves, the American flag, the Capitol building — use photographic source material as the basis for technically virtuosic drawing. In the dataset, Study of Eric sold at 14.7× estimate, $441k against a $30k estimate — the dataset's top outperformer.

famous_works.title: Men in the Cities
famous_works.notes: A series of monumental charcoal drawings of figures in business attire, contorted as if by an unseen force. The canonical images of 1980s alienation — reproduced as wallpaper in American Psycho.

famous_works.title: Untitled (Cloud for Patrick)
famous_works.notes: A six-foot mushroom cloud rendered in charcoal with photographic precision. The nuclear sublime — scale, clarity, and dread — as hyperrealist drawing.

famous_works.title: Untitled (After Goya)
famous_works.notes: A hyperrealist charcoal response to Goya's Saturn Devouring His Son. The art-historical source reprocessed through Longo's documentary technique.

lesser_known_works.title: Study of Eric
lesser_known_works.notes: A Men in the Cities figure study — the 14.7× outperformer in the dataset. The preparatory studies for the series carry an immediacy that the final monumental drawings formalise.

lesser_known_works.title: Empirical House
lesser_known_works.notes: A work from Longo's more installation-oriented period — objects and images combined in a theatrical environment. Less well-known than the drawing series.

lesser_known_works.title: Sword of the Pig
lesser_known_works.notes: A relief sculpture from the end of the Men in the Cities period — the move from drawing into three dimensions, from representation into object.

artists.bio: Born in Honfleur, Normandy, the son of a sailor, Boudin ran a stationery and framing shop in Le Havre before the artists Troyon, Millet, and Couture encouraged him to paint full-time. He was the painter who introduced the young Claude Monet to plein-air technique on the Norman coast in 1858 — his influence on the artist who would become the centre of Impressionism cannot be overstated. His beach scenes at Trouville (from 1862 onward) — fashionable figures in crinolines against vast sky, the meteorological accuracy of the Normandy atmosphere — established the subject matter that Monet, Renoir, and Sisley would elaborate. Jongkind and Boudin are now understood as the essential pre-Impressionist generation. His work enters the market with reliable strength: collector enthusiasm for 19th-century French plein-air masters — particularly sky studies — has grown substantially since the 2000s.

famous_works.title: The Beach at Trouville
famous_works.notes: Fashionable figures with parasols against a luminous sky. The Impressionist programme — sensation of light and atmosphere rather than narrative — is fully present a decade before the 1874 exhibition.

famous_works.title: Jetty and Wharf at Trouville
famous_works.notes: The harbour infrastructure depicted with documentary precision against a dramatic Normandy sky. Marine painting and meteorology as dual subjects.

famous_works.title: Washerwomen near Trouville
famous_works.notes: Working-class figures on the Norman coast rather than the bourgeois resort. Boudin's range beyond the fashionable beach — the labour behind the leisure economy.

lesser_known_works.title: Study of Sky (Étude de ciel)
lesser_known_works.notes: Rapid plein-air sky studies — Boudin made hundreds. In the dataset, a study sold at 10.24× estimate; the sky study format is among the most sought-after of his work.

lesser_known_works.title: Fishing Boats at Berck
lesser_known_works.notes: The northern French coast rather than Normandy — Boudin's range extended to Pas-de-Calais. The boats and the sky in equal balance.

lesser_known_works.title: The Harbour at Antwerp
lesser_known_works.notes: Industrial port imagery — the steamships and loading docks that Boudin observed on his travels to Belgium. The modernity of the industrial port is acknowledged rather than avoided.

top_lot.title: Étude de ciel
top_lot.sale_name: Impressionist and Modern Art Day and Works on Paper Sale

artists.bio: Born in Macklin, Saskatchewan, Canada, Martin moved to New York after studies in Oregon and Columbia University, where she taught. She developed her mature practice in the early 1960s: small-scale canvases of pencil-drawn grids on white or off-white paint, the lines hand-drawn with a ruler, carrying the slight irregularity of the human hand within a rigid structure. She was associated with the first generation of Minimalists at Elsworth Kelly's building on Coenties Slip in Lower Manhattan, though she rejected the Minimalist label, insisting on the emotional and spiritual dimension of her work. She left New York abruptly in 1967 following the death of Ad Reinhardt and a breakdown, and moved to New Mexico, where she lived in self-built adobe houses and did not paint for six years. The late works are horizontal bands of pale colour — pink, blue, grey — of extraordinary refinement.

famous_works.title: The Islands I–XII
famous_works.notes: A series of small square canvases — the grid at its most intimate and complete. The islands of the title are the canvases themselves.

famous_works.title: With My Back to the World
famous_works.notes: A late work of pale horizontal bands. The title is a statement of position — withdrawal from the social world as the condition of the work.

famous_works.title: Friendship
famous_works.notes: Gold leaf on canvas crossed by thin pencil grid lines. The warmth of the gold and the severity of the grid — the emotional and structural in productive tension.

lesser_known_works.title: Happy Holiday
lesser_known_works.notes: A late canvas of yellow bands — an unusually warm, light work for Martin. The title's unironic positivity and the painting's quiet pleasure are matching.

lesser_known_works.title: Grass
lesser_known_works.notes: One of the last works before the New Mexico departure — thin horizontal lines on pale ground. The landscape of the title is implicit in the colour and the horizon.

lesser_known_works.title: Untitled #10
lesser_known_works.notes: A return to grid format after the horizontal-bands period of the 1970s and 1980s. The grid reinterpreted in softer, more atmospheric terms than the early Manhattan works.

artists.bio: Born in Knoxville, Tennessee, Gribbon studied at the School of Visual Arts in New York. She paints domestic interiors and intimate moments — her partner and family in scenes of reading, sleeping, working, watching television — with a clear art-historical awareness (references to Vallotton, Hopper, and Vermeer are structurally present) but a contemporary frankness that is distinctly her own. The paintings insist on the ordinary as a subject worthy of the sustained attention traditionally reserved for the monumental: a woman watching a screen, a figure reflected in a window, a child asleep in an armchair. The light is specific and photographic but the paint handling is deliberate and Old Master in its patience. Her market trajectory has been rapid: in the dataset, Set List Focus sold at 8.06× estimate — one of the most dramatic outperformances — signalling strong speculative and institutional interest.

famous_works.title: Set List Focus
famous_works.notes: A figure in intimate domestic light — the 8.06× outperformer in the dataset. The painting's quietness makes the market response the loudest element of its history.

famous_works.title: It Doesn't Always Look Like This
famous_works.notes: A self-portrait reflected in a window, the sitter's gaze directed at the viewer through the glass. Vermeer's window light processed through a contemporary domestic moment.

famous_works.title: A Whole New Kind of Morning
famous_works.notes: Her partner reads in morning light — the quiet moment rendered with the formal attention of a seventeenth-century genre painting translated into a present-tense domestic space.

lesser_known_works.title: Lit at Night
lesser_known_works.notes: A figure illuminated by a screen in a dark room. The contemporary light source that Hopper's windows have been replaced by — the painting thinks about what has changed.

lesser_known_works.title: The Voyager Has Landed
lesser_known_works.notes: An early work that established the intimate scale and domestic subject matter before the larger canvases of the mature period.

lesser_known_works.title: Slipping Into Fiction
lesser_known_works.notes: A figure absorbed in a book — the act of reading as the subject of a painting. The reader disappearing into language while the painter watches.

artists.bio: Born in San Francisco, Serra worked in steel mills during college summers — the material knowledge that became his artistic medium. He studied at UC Santa Barbara, UC Berkeley, and Yale (BA and MFA), then in Florence and Paris on a Yale traveling fellowship. His early props and lead splashes (1969) established a practice in which the physical properties of materials and the force of gravity are the subject: lead rolled from one direction meets itself, propped sheets lean against walls, and weight and balance are made simultaneously visible and precarious. His mature large-scale weathered-steel sculptures — the Torqued Ellipses (from 1996) and the East-West/West-East installation in Qatar (2014) — create experiences of enclosed and open space that make the body's movement through space the work. He remained active until his death in March 2024.

famous_works.title: Tilted Arc
famous_works.notes: A curved weathered steel wall installed in Federal Plaza, New York. Removed in 1989 after a public controversy — the most contested public art installation in American history.

famous_works.title: Torqued Ellipses I, II and III
famous_works.notes: Three steel sculptures whose interior and exterior describe different spatial experiences simultaneously. Made possible only with computer assistance — the first large works where digital design preceded physical fabrication.

famous_works.title: The Matter of Time
famous_works.notes: Eight massive sculptures permanently installed in the Guggenheim Bilbao. Walking through the installation is to walk through a spatial autobiography of Serra's formal preoccupations over forty years.

lesser_known_works.title: Splash Piece: Casting
lesser_known_works.notes: Molten lead thrown into the junction of floor and wall, then removed, creating a negative cast of the angle. The corner of a room as sculpture; process as form.

lesser_known_works.title: Hand Catching Lead
lesser_known_works.notes: A film of a hand catching and failing to catch falling lead splinters. Process documented rather than frozen — the action as the work.

lesser_known_works.title: Circuit
lesser_known_works.notes: Four steel plates placed in the four corners of a large room, visible only from specific positions. The room's geometry activated by the plates' placement — the space of the gallery as material.

artists.bio: Born in Port Arthur, Texas, Rauschenberg studied at Black Mountain College under Josef Albers and John Cage before arriving in New York. He developed the 'Combines' (1954–64) — works that incorporated objects from daily life (stuffed animals, tires, clothing, photographs, street detritus) into painterly surfaces, making the distinction between painting and sculpture moot. He was among the first artists to collaborate with choreographers (Merce Cunningham, Paul Taylor) and the first to explore the relationship between art and technology institutionally (Experiments in Art and Technology, 1966). He won the Venice Biennale Grand Prize in 1964 — the moment American Pop and Conceptual art were officially consecrated by the European establishment. His Tibetan Buddhism, his gay identity (publicly declared), and his Texan populism were all present in the work, which remains among the most generative in post-war American art.

famous_works.title: Bed
famous_works.notes: His own quilt, pillow, and sheet hung vertically on a stretcher and smeared with toothpaste, paint, and nail varnish. The painting as domestic object, the domestic object as painting.

famous_works.title: Monogram
famous_works.notes: A stuffed angora goat with a tire around its middle, standing on a painted combine canvas. The most famous of the Combines — sculpture, painting, and absurdist theatre simultaneously.

famous_works.title: Erased de Kooning Drawing
famous_works.notes: Rauschenberg visited de Kooning and asked for a drawing he could erase. De Kooning chose a difficult, multiply-media drawing. The act of erasure as the art — Dada's destruction turned into a relationship.

lesser_known_works.title: White Paintings
lesser_known_works.notes: Pure white canvases that catch shadow and light — precursors to John Cage's 4'33" in their creation of a field for ambient incident rather than a determined composition.

lesser_known_works.title: Barge
lesser_known_works.notes: A monumental transfer drawing — newspaper and magazine images transferred to canvas using solvent. The 22-foot composite of American imagery is the most ambitious single surface in the transfer series.

lesser_known_works.title: Retroactive I
lesser_known_works.notes: Kennedy's photograph layered with an astronaut and a hand-signaling figure. The transfer technique applied to the imagery of the 1960s American moment — assassination and space race in the same picture.
